category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Partial Differential Equations/Laplace and Poisson Equations

questiontext.text: A function \(u\) is harmonic in a domain \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n\) if and only if:

feedback.text: Correct. A harmonic function satisfies Laplace's equation Δu=0. Harmonic functions are infinitely differentiable (analytic in fact) in the interior of their domain.
Δu=1 defines a solution of Poisson's equation, not Laplace's. Harmonic functions satisfy the homogeneous equation Δu=0.
Vanishing on the boundary is a boundary condition, not the definition of harmonicity. A harmonic function satisfies Δu=0 in the interior, regardless of boundary values.
|∇u|² = 0 implies u is constant, which is a special case of harmonic but not the general definition. Harmonic functions need only satisfy Δu=0.

questiontext.text: The mean value property for harmonic functions states: if \(\Delta u = 0\) in \(\Omega\), then for any ball \(B(x_0,r) \Subset \Omega\):

feedback.text: Correct. A harmonic function equals both its spherical mean (average over the sphere) and its ball mean (average over the ball). This property is equivalent to harmonicity (it characterizes harmonic functions).
The maximum principle says harmonic functions attain their maximum on the boundary — but not that u(x₀) equals the maximum. The mean value property says u(x₀) equals the average, not the maximum.
Harmonic functions need not vanish at interior points. The mean value property equates u(x₀) with the average of u over nearby spheres/balls.
Missing the normalization: the spherical average divides by the surface area |∂B_r| = ωₙrⁿ⁻¹, not just r.

questiontext.text: The strong maximum principle for harmonic functions states:

answer.text: If \(\Delta u = 0\) in a connected domain \(\Omega\) and \(u\) attains its maximum in the interior, then \(u\) is constant.

feedback.text: Correct. The strong maximum principle: if a harmonic function attains its maximum (or minimum) at an interior point of a connected domain, it must be constant. Nonconstant harmonic functions attain their extrema only on the boundary.

answer.text: A harmonic function in \(\Omega\) is always nonnegative.

feedback.text: Harmonic functions can take any sign. The maximum principle concerns where extrema are attained, not their sign.

answer.text: The maximum of a harmonic function on \(\overline{\Omega}\) is attained at every boundary point.

feedback.text: The maximum is attained on the boundary (not necessarily at every boundary point). The strong maximum principle says it cannot be attained in the interior (unless the function is constant).

answer.text: Harmonic functions have no local maxima anywhere.

feedback.text: More precisely: a nonconstant harmonic function has no local maxima in the interior of its domain. It can attain global maxima on the boundary.

questiontext.text: The fundamental solution of the Laplace equation \(\Delta \Phi = \delta(x)\) in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) (\(n \geq 2\)) is:

feedback.text: Correct. In 2D: Φ=ln|x|/(2π). In 3D: Φ=−1/(4π|x|). In n≥3: Φ=−1/(n(n−2)ωₙ)|x|^{2−n} where ωₙ is the volume of the unit ball. Note sign conventions vary; some use +1/(n(n-2)ωₙ) with Δ(−Φ)=δ.
e^{−|x|} is a radial function but ΔΦ ≠ δ(x). The fundamental solution has algebraic singularity (polynomial in |x|), not exponential.
|x|²/(2n) satisfies Δ(|x|²/(2n))=1 (constant), so it solves Δu=1, not Δu=δ(x). This is the Newtonian potential producing a uniform charge distribution.
Close, but the sign convention varies. The standard fundamental solution in ℝ³ satisfies Δ(−1/(4π|x|))=δ(x). The answer here has positive sign, which corresponds to Δ(1/(4π|x|))=−δ(x).

questiontext.text: The Poisson kernel for the unit disk \(B(0,1) \subset \mathbb{R}^2\) with boundary data \(u=f\) on \(\partial B\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. The Poisson integral formula for the disk: u = (1/2π)∫P(r,θ−φ)f(φ)dφ where P(r,θ)=(1−r²)/(1−2r cosθ+r²) is the Poisson kernel. It can be derived from Green's function or Fourier series.
This gives the mean value (the value at r=0), valid only at the center. For r>0, the Poisson kernel gives a non-trivial weighted average.
This is not the correct Poisson formula. The kernel is (1−r²)/(1−2r cos(θ−φ)+r²), not r²/(1−r²).

answer.text: \(u(r,\theta) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{f}_n r^{|n|} e^{in\theta}\) without an integral formula

feedback.text: The Fourier series expansion is valid and equivalent to the Poisson integral, but the question asks for the integral formula. Both are correct representations.

questiontext.text: Uniqueness for the Dirichlet problem \(\Delta u = f\) in \(\Omega\), \(u = g\) on \(\partial\Omega\) follows from:

answer.text: The maximum principle: if \(\Delta v = 0\) in \(\Omega\) and \(v = 0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), then \(v \equiv 0\) in \(\Omega\).

feedback.text: Correct. If u₁ and u₂ are two solutions, then v=u₁−u₂ satisfies Δv=0 with v=0 on ∂Ω. By the maximum principle, max v = max_{∂Ω} v = 0, and similarly min v=0, so v≡0. This gives uniqueness.

answer.text: The Lax-Milgram theorem applied to the weak formulation.

feedback.text: Lax-Milgram provides existence and uniqueness in the weak (Sobolev space) setting, but the classical uniqueness argument uses the maximum principle directly.

answer.text: The Cauchy-Kovalevskaya theorem guarantees a unique analytic solution.

feedback.text: CK applies to analytic Cauchy problems. For the Dirichlet problem (elliptic), uniqueness follows from the maximum principle, not CK.

answer.text: Uniqueness fails for the Dirichlet problem in general domains.

feedback.text: Uniqueness holds for the Dirichlet problem on bounded domains with smooth (or even Lipschitz) boundaries, by the maximum principle.

questiontext.text: Green's second identity states: for smooth \(u, v\) on \(\overline{\Omega}\),

answer.text: \(\displaystyle\int_\Omega (u\Delta v - v\Delta u)\,dx = \displaystyle\oint_{\partial\Omega}\!\left(u\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial\nu} - v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial\nu}\right)dS\)

feedback.text: Correct. Green's second identity follows from the divergence theorem applied to u∇v−v∇u. Setting Δu=0 and v=Φ (fundamental solution) yields the representation formula for harmonic functions.
This is Green's first identity (with v=u in the first identity), not the second identity. Green's second identity involves the antisymmetric combination uΔv−vΔu.
Missing the boundary term ∮ u(∂v/∂ν)dS. Green's first identity is ∫∇u·∇v dx = −∫uΔv dx + ∮u(∂v/∂ν)dS.
This would only hold if the boundary terms vanish (e.g., with compact support). Green's second identity includes the boundary integral involving normal derivatives.

questiontext.text: The Green's function representation for the Poisson equation \(-\Delta u = f\) in \(\Omega\), \(u=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\) gives:

answer.text: \(u(x) = \displaystyle\int_\Omega G(x,y)\,f(y)\,dy\), where \(G(x,y) = \Phi(x-y) - h^x(y)\) and \(\Delta_y h^x = 0\), \(h^x = \Phi(x-\cdot)\) on \(\partial\Omega\).

feedback.text: Correct. The Green's function G(x,y) for the domain Ω is the fundamental solution Φ corrected by h^x (the harmonic correction that makes G=0 on ∂Ω). The solution is u(x)=∫G(x,y)f(y)dy.
This formula gives the Newton potential, which solves −Δu=f on ℝⁿ but does not satisfy the Dirichlet BC u=0 on ∂Ω. The Green's function adds the harmonic correction h^x.
This boundary integral formula gives u in terms of boundary data, applicable when f=0 (homogeneous Poisson) and data on ∂Ω. For a volume source f, the volume integral is needed.
This form (obtained by integration by parts) is valid under additional regularity on f, but the direct formula u=∫G(x,y)f(y)dy is the standard representation.

questiontext.text: The Green's function for the unit ball \(B(0,1)\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) is (using the method of images):

answer.text: \(G(x,y) = \dfrac{-1}{4\pi|x-y|} + \dfrac{1}{4\pi|x||y^*-y|}\), where \(y^* = y/|y|^2\) is the inversion of \(y\) in \(\partial B\).

feedback.text: Correct. The image point of y∈B is y*=y/|y|² (outside B). The Green's function G(x,y)=Φ(x−y)−Φ(|y|(x−y*)) satisfies −ΔG=δ_y and G=0 on ∂B, using the Kelvin image method.
The fundamental solution alone does not vanish on ∂B. The Green's function requires a corrector (image) term to satisfy G=0 on the boundary.
The logarithmic formula is the 2D fundamental solution. In 3D, the fundamental solution is −1/(4π|x−y|).
This is related to the normal derivative of G (the Poisson kernel, which appears in the boundary integral formula), not the Green's function itself.

questiontext.text: Liouville's theorem for harmonic functions states:

answer.text: Every bounded harmonic function on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is constant.

feedback.text: Correct. If Δu=0 on ℝⁿ and |u|≤M, then u is constant. This follows from the gradient estimate |∇u(x)|≤nM/r (valid for any ball B(x,r) by differentiating the mean value formula), letting r→∞.

answer.text: Every harmonic function on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is a polynomial.

feedback.text: Harmonic polynomials exist (e.g., u=x₁²−x₂² in ℝ²), but general harmonic functions on ℝⁿ are not polynomials. Liouville's theorem concerns bounded harmonic functions being constant.

answer.text: Harmonic functions on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) cannot have compact support unless they are zero.

feedback.text: This is also a consequence of the mean value property (or Liouville's theorem), but Liouville's theorem is specifically about bounded implies constant. A compactly supported harmonic function being trivial is a separate (related) result.

Real-analyticity is a correct property of harmonic functions (follows from Poisson integral), but it is not Liouville's theorem. Liouville's theorem is specifically about bounded harmonic functions being constant.

questiontext.text: The Neumann problem \(\Delta u = f\) in \(\Omega\), \(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial\nu} = g\) on \(\partial\Omega\) has a solution if and only if:

feedback.text: Correct. Integrating Δu=f over Ω and applying the divergence theorem: ∫_Ω f dx = ∫_Ω Δu dx = ∮_{∂Ω} (∂u/∂ν) dS = ∮ g dS. So ∫f dx = ∮g dS is a necessary condition. The Neumann problem is unique up to a constant.
The compatibility condition involves both f and g: ∫_Ω f dx = ∮_{∂Ω} g dS, not just ∫f=0.

answer.text: There is no compatibility condition; the Neumann problem always has a solution.

feedback.text: The compatibility condition ∫f+∮g=0 is necessary. Without it, no solution exists (by integrating the equation over Ω and applying the divergence theorem).
g=0 makes ∮g=0, which satisfies the compatibility only if also ∫f=0. The general condition is ∫f+∮g=0, allowing nonzero g.

questiontext.text: Elliptic regularity for \(-\Delta u = f\) in \(\Omega\) states: if \(f \in H^k(\Omega)\) (Sobolev space), then in the interior:

answer.text: \(u \in H^{k+2}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\Omega)\), gaining two derivatives relative to \(f\).

feedback.text: Correct. Elliptic regularity (interior): if f∈H^k_loc, then u∈H^{k+2}_loc. This "gain of two derivatives" is the hallmark of elliptic equations. In particular, f∈L² gives u∈H²_loc, and f∈C^∞ gives u∈C^∞ (smoothness propagates).
Elliptic equations gain regularity. u is two Sobolev orders smoother than f (in the interior), not the same order.
This is what happens for HYPERBOLIC equations (which lose regularity). Elliptic equations gain two derivatives. For the Laplacian, u is smoother than f.

answer.text: No regularity gain: \(u\) has the same regularity as \(f\) since \(\Delta\) is a second-order operator.

feedback.text: The Laplacian is second order but elliptic, so it improves regularity. This contrasts with hyperbolic operators which do not improve regularity.

questiontext.text: Harnack's inequality for positive harmonic functions states: if \(u > 0\), \(\Delta u = 0\) in \(B(0,R)\), then for any \(|x| \leq r < R\):

feedback.text: Correct. Harnack's inequality bounds a positive harmonic function in terms of its value at the center. In particular, it shows that positive harmonic functions cannot have isolated zeros and implies equicontinuity for bounded families.

answer.text: \(u(x) = u(0)\) for all \(x\) (positive harmonic functions are constant).

feedback.text: Positive harmonic functions are not necessarily constant on bounded domains. Liouville's theorem (bounded harmonic on ℝⁿ is constant) requires global boundedness, not just positivity.
Harnack's inequality is a two-sided multiplicative bound, not a Lipschitz difference estimate. It states C₁u(0) ≤ u(x) ≤ C₂u(0) for appropriate constants.
The maximum is on the boundary (maximum principle), so u(0) is not necessarily the maximum. Harnack's inequality provides a two-sided bound involving ratios.

questiontext.text: The spherical harmonics \(Y_\ell^m(\theta,\phi)\) on \(S^2\) are eigenfunctions of the Laplace-Beltrami operator \(\Delta_{S^2}\) with eigenvalues:

feedback.text: Correct. Spherical harmonics are eigenfunctions of the spherical Laplacian with eigenvalue −ℓ(ℓ+1). For each ℓ there are 2ℓ+1 independent spherical harmonics (m=−ℓ,...,ℓ). They arise from separating Laplace's equation in spherical coordinates.
The Laplace-Beltrami operator on S² is negative semi-definite; its eigenvalues are −ℓ(ℓ+1) ≤ 0, not +ℓ(ℓ+1) > 0.
The eigenvalue is −ℓ(ℓ+1), not −ℓ². These differ for all ℓ≥2.

answer.text: Spherical harmonics are not eigenfunctions of any differential operator.

feedback.text: Spherical harmonics are precisely the eigenfunctions of the Laplace-Beltrami operator Δ_{S²}. This is their defining property in the context of harmonic analysis on the sphere.

questiontext.text: The Dirichlet principle states that the solution \(u\) of \(-\Delta u = 0\) in \(\Omega\), \(u = g\) on \(\partial\Omega\) minimizes the Dirichlet energy over all functions with the same boundary data:

answer.text: \(E[u] = \displaystyle\int_\Omega |\nabla u|^2\,dx \leq \displaystyle\int_\Omega |\nabla v|^2\,dx\) for all \(v\) with \(v|_{\partial\Omega} = g\).

feedback.text: Correct. The harmonic function u minimizes the Dirichlet energy ∫|∇u|²dx among all functions with boundary data g. This is proven using Green's first identity: ∫|∇(u−v)|² = ∫|∇u|²−∫|∇v|²+2∫(u−v)Δu dx... The key is that u is harmonic so the cross term vanishes.
The Dirichlet energy is ∫|∇u|² (involving gradients), not ∫u² (L² norm). The harmonic function minimizes the gradient energy, not the L² norm.
The harmonic function minimizes (not maximizes) the Dirichlet energy. This is the variational characterization of Laplace's equation.

answer.text: The Dirichlet energy is conserved along solutions: \(E[u(\cdot,t)] = E[u(\cdot,0)]\).

feedback.text: This statement concerns time evolution (wave equation). The Dirichlet principle is a static variational characterization of harmonic functions.

questiontext.text: The Kelvin transform of a harmonic function \(u\) in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) (\(n \geq 3\)) is defined as \(u^*(x) = |x|^{2-n}u(x^*)\) where \(x^* = x/|x|^2\). The Kelvin transform is:

answer.text: Also harmonic (harmonic in the inverted domain), allowing extension of results from bounded to unbounded domains.

feedback.text: Correct. The Kelvin transform preserves harmonicity: Δu=0 iff Δu*=0 (in the appropriate domain). This is used to convert exterior problems to interior problems and to study harmonic functions near infinity.

answer.text: A solution of Poisson's equation \(\Delta u^* = f^*\) for some source \(f^*\).

feedback.text: If u is harmonic (Δu=0), then u* is also harmonic (Δu*=0). The Kelvin transform of a harmonic function is harmonic.

answer.text: Not harmonic; it satisfies a different elliptic equation.

feedback.text: The Kelvin transform of a harmonic function is harmonic. This is the key property of the Kelvin transform.
The Kelvin transform works in any dimension n≥2: u*(x)=|x|^{2−n}u(x/|x|²). In 2D the exponent is 2−2=0 so it simplifies to u*(x)=u(x/|x|²).

questiontext.text: Bôcher's theorem (removable singularity for harmonic functions) states: if \(\Delta u = 0\) in a punctured ball \(B(0,r) \setminus \{0\}\) in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) (\(n \geq 2\)) and \(u(x) = o(|x|^{2-n})\) as \(x \to 0\) (for \(n \geq 3\)), then:

answer.text: The singularity at 0 is removable: \(u\) extends to a harmonic function on the full ball \(B(0,r)\).

feedback.text: Correct. If a harmonic function in a punctured ball grows slower than the fundamental solution |x|^{2−n}, the singularity is removable (the function extends harmonically through 0). Singularities that grow like |x|^{2−n} correspond to a point charge (fundamental solution) and are not removable.

The condition u=o(|x|^{2−n}) means u grows slower than the fundamental solution. This is precisely the condition under which the singularity is removable (u stays bounded or grows very slowly). Not all harmonic functions in punctured balls blow up.
The removability theorem says u extends to a harmonic function on the full ball, not that it must be zero. Any harmonic function on the full ball satisfies u=o(|x|^{2−n}) near 0.

answer.text: \(u\) must equal the fundamental solution: \(u(x) = c|x|^{2-n}\).

feedback.text: The fundamental solution |x|^{2−n} is NOT o(|x|^{2−n}) (it's exactly O(|x|^{2−n})). The condition u=o(|x|^{2−n}) rules out the fundamental solution type singularity, making it removable.

questiontext.text: Perron's method for solving the Dirichlet problem \(\Delta u = 0\) in \(\Omega\), \(u=g\) on \(\partial\Omega\) uses the Perron solution:

answer.text: \(u(x) = \sup\{v(x) : v \text{ subharmonic in }\Omega,\; v \leq g \text{ on }\partial\Omega\}\)

feedback.text: Correct. The Perron solution is the supremum of all subharmonic minorants of g. It is harmonic in Ω and takes boundary values g at "regular" boundary points (where a barrier exists). Perron's method works for general continuous boundary data on sufficiently regular domains.

answer.text: \(u(x) = \inf\{v(x) : v \text{ superharmonic in }\Omega,\; v \geq g \text{ on }\partial\Omega\}\)

feedback.text: Both the sup of subharmonic minorants and the inf of superharmonic majorants equal the Perron solution. The standard definition uses the supremum of subharmonic functions ≤ g on ∂Ω.
This is the Dirichlet energy minimization approach, not Perron's method. Perron's method uses the Perron-Wiener-Brelot supremum construction, independent of variational principles.

answer.text: Solving the PDE iteratively using finite differences.

feedback.text: Finite differences are a numerical method. Perron's method is an analytic existence proof using subharmonic functions and does not involve numerical iteration.

questiontext.text: A function \(v\) is subharmonic in \(\Omega\) if \(\Delta v \geq 0\). For a subharmonic function, the mean value inequality states:

feedback.text: Correct. Subharmonic functions satisfy a sub-mean-value property: the value at a point is at most the average over any surrounding sphere. This is analogous to a convex function being below any chord.
This is the property of superharmonic functions (Δv≤0). Subharmonic functions satisfy the inequality with ≤ (sub-mean-value), not ≥.
Equality holds for harmonic functions (Δv=0). Subharmonic functions (Δv≥0) satisfy v(x₀) ≤ average (strict inequality in general).

answer.text: Subharmonic functions satisfy a maximum principle: they attain their maximum in the interior.

feedback.text: This is the opposite of the correct statement. Subharmonic functions satisfy the MAXIMUM principle in the form: the maximum is attained on the boundary (not the interior), since the value at any interior point is ≤ the boundary average.

questiontext.text: For the exterior Dirichlet problem \(\Delta u = 0\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3 \setminus \overline{B(0,1)}\), \(u=g\) on \(|x|=1\), and \(u(x) \to 0\) as \(|x| \to \infty\), the solution is given by:

answer.text: \(u(x) = \dfrac{1}{|x|}\,v\!\left(\dfrac{x}{|x|^2}\right)\) where \(v\) solves the interior Dirichlet problem on \(B(0,1)\) with data \(g\).

feedback.text: Correct. The Kelvin transform u*(x)=|x|^{−1}v(x/|x|²) converts the exterior problem to an interior problem in B(0,1). The condition u→0 at infinity becomes v(0)=0 (regularity at the origin for the interior problem).

answer.text: The exterior problem has no solution since \(\mathbb{R}^3\setminus\overline{B}\) is unbounded.

feedback.text: Exterior Dirichlet problems do have solutions (for n≥3 with appropriate decay conditions at infinity). The Kelvin transform reduces them to interior problems.

answer.text: \(u(x) = g(x/|x|)\) (just the boundary data radially extended).

feedback.text: Radial extension g(x/|x|) is constant on radial lines but is not harmonic in general. The harmonic solution requires the Poisson kernel for the exterior problem.

answer.text: Uniqueness fails for the exterior problem due to the unbounded domain.

feedback.text: With the decay condition u→0 at infinity, uniqueness holds for the exterior problem. Without this condition, there would be infinitely many solutions (e.g., u + harmonic function vanishing on ∂B and at infinity).
